# The Foundation

## Open Collective Europe – Legal Information

### Legal Status

**Open Collective Europe Foundation** is a **Belgian public utility foundation** (*Fondation d'utilité publique*), legally registered to operate across Europe and internationally.

* **Company number**: 0803922736
* **VAT number**: BE0803922736
* **PIC number** (for EU funding): 883724806
* **Registered address**: Avenue Louise 500, boîte 205, 1050 Brussels, Belgium

***

### Legal Documents

Key legal documents — including the Foundation’s statutes (in French and English), the royal decree of recognition, and incorporation paperwork — are available [here](https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/15Mu5fuQZGVi0IMCS8pBg487MGcQyc8Rk?usp=drive_link)

* Terms and Conditions for hosted Collectives [here](https://docs.google.com/document/d/e/2PACX-1vS2jTb55T-kXHDPLNWVg4xUuMGmediT9VBqwsNpdQA8geRpcPsyYvfPggwJ6iZaPw/pub)
* Overview of foundation status and obligations in Belgium *(optional internal GitBook link)*

***

### OCE Partnerships

### Partnerships

Open Collective Europe collaborates with a broad network of organizations to support collectives, streamline cross-border grantmaking, and ensure compliance across jurisdictions.

#### Platform and ecosystem partnerships

* **Open Finance Consortium (OFICO)** \
  We work closely with the team behind the Open Collective platform, which powers our financial and collaborative infrastructure. This partnership ensures that OCE’s services are seamlessly integrated with the platform’s tools and features.
* **Open Source Collective (OSC)**\
  A US-based nonprofit fiscal host focused on open source communities. We share platform practices and collaborate on shared needs across the hosting ecosystem, especially for tech-focused initiatives.

#### Financial and compliance infrastructure

* **King Baudouin Foundation (KBF)**\
  Our partnership with KBF enables recognized charitable status across Europe and the US. This makes it possible for donors — including individuals and institutional funders — to benefit from tax-deductibility when contributing to eligible initiatives.
* **Myriad USA**\
  Open Collective Europe collaborates with Myriad USA to facilitate **tax-deductible donations from U.S.-based donors**.

{% hint style="warning" %}
Collectives can't directly contact Myriad USA&#x20;
{% endhint %}

***

Open Collective Europe positions itself as a trusted intermediary between **unincorporated, mission-driven collectives** and **international funders**, especially US-based foundations wishing to support initiatives in Europe, Africa, and Asia. Our partnerships are designed to make cross-border philanthropy more accessible, compliant, and efficient.

***

### Historical Context

Open Collective Europe Foundation was established independently in Belgium.\
It later **absorbed and replaced** the former regional fiscal hosts:

* **Open Collective Paris (FR)**
* **Open Collective Brussels (BE)**

These legacy operations are now fully integrated into OCE.

***

### Contact

For any questions about our legal structure, documentation, or fiscal status:\
📧 <oce@opencollective.com>


# Meet the Open Collective Europe Team

### How we work

Open Collective Europe is run by a small, multilingual, and fully remote team based primarily in Europe.\
We operate asynchronously across time zones, and value transparency, autonomy, and collaboration in our day-to-day work.

Our goal is to provide trusted and accessible infrastructure to communities and funders who share our vision for the commons.

***

### Core Team

* **Jean-François De Hertogh**\
  Executive Director\
  Leads the foundation’s strategy, partnerships, and governance.
* **Ivan Cukeric**\
  Financial Operations\
  Handles daily financial processes, expense reviews, fund transfers, and reporting.
* **Laura Manne**\
  Grant & Operations Lead

***

### Foundation Board

The foundation is legally administered by three board members:

* **Jean-François De Hertogh**
* **Pia Mancini**
* **Shannon Wray**


# Director (JF)

### 1. Purpose of the Role

The Director is responsible for the overall direction, integrity, and reliability of Open Collective Europe.

The role ensures that the foundation operates:

* in line with its mission
* with a high level of trust and compliance
* with clear structure, accountability, and scalability

The Director acts as the final point of responsibility across all areas of the organization.

***

### 2. Core Responsibilities

#### 2.1 Strategic Direction

* Define and drive the strategic positioning of the foundation
* Develop relationships with:
  * partner foundations
  * major donors
  * institutional stakeholders
* Identify and develop key opportunities for growth and impact

***

#### 2.2 Financial Oversight

* Ensure overall financial integrity of the organization
* Supervise:
  * budget preparation
  * annual accounts
  * audit processes
* Work closely with:
  * external CFO
  * accountant
* Validate:
  * high-value transactions
  * exceptional financial decisions

***

#### 2.3 Organizational Structure & Delegation

* Define roles, responsibilities, and internal processes
* Ensure that operational responsibilities are clearly assigned and understood, in particular:
  * day-to-day operations to the Operations & Support Associate
  * advanced operations and coordination to the Operations & Coordination role
* Ensure operations are:
  * reliable
  * scalable
  * well-structured
* Identify gaps and implement improvements

***

#### 2.4 Oversight of Operations

* Ensure that operations are executed according to defined standards and processes
* Put in place appropriate validation mechanisms for critical operations
* Maintain visibility over:
  * financial operations
  * platform configuration
  * key interactions with collectives
* Intervene selectively when:
  * a situation is complex or high-risk
  * an issue requires escalation
  * a decision is needed

> The role is not to systematically review or correct operational work, but to ensure that systems and responsibilities are in place to guarantee quality.

***

#### 2.5 Final Validation & Risk Management

* Act as final decision-maker on:
  * sensitive financial matters
  * exceptions and special agreements
  * high-risk situations
* Ensure risks are identified and mitigated

***

#### 2.6 External Relations

* Represent the foundation externally
* Maintain relationships with:
  * key projects (e.g. GSF)
  * strategic partners
  * major stakeholders
* Ensure alignment between messaging and positioning

***

#### 2.7 Team Oversight

* Supervise team members and role evolution
* Ensure clarity of expectations and responsibilities
* Provide direction and feedback when needed

***

### 3. Operating Principles

#### 3.1 Accountability

The Director retains ultimate responsibility for:

* financial accuracy
* operational reliability
* external relationships

***

#### 3.2 Delegation & Responsibility

Operational responsibilities are delegated to the team with clear ownership.

Each role is expected to:

* execute tasks independently
* ensure accuracy and completeness
* take responsibility for the quality of their work

The Director:

* defines the framework
* ensures accountability
* does not act as a systematic validation layer

> Recurring need for correction or supervision indicates a need to adjust roles, processes, or responsibilities.

***

#### 3.3 Clarity over Informality

The organization should not rely on informal corrections or implicit knowledge.

The Director ensures:

* clear processes
* documented expectations
* structured decision-making

***

#### 3.4 Risk Awareness

All critical operations must be:

* understood
* monitored
* secured

***

### 4. Scope & Positioning

The Director:

* leads strategy and structure
* validates critical decisions
* ensures overall coherence

While the Director may remain involved in certain operational aspects, these are delegated by default and should not rely on continuous direct execution.

***

### 5. Expected Outcomes

* a reliable and structured organization
* strong trust from partners and collectives
* clear delegation and accountability across the team
* reduced dependency on the Director for day-to-day operations
* operations that function without systematic correction


# Financial Compliance & Platform Specialist (Ivan)

{% hint style="info" %}
Current Position: Financial Compliance & Platform Specialist

Reporting Line: Reports to the Executive Director
{% endhint %}

**1. Purpose of the Role**

Ivan is the Guardian of Integrity and Financial Accuracy for the Foundation. His mission is to ensure that every transaction on the platform is legally sound and perfectly reconciled for accounting purposes. He acts as the operational bridge between the Open Collective platform and our external financial partners (Accountant & CFO).

**2. Key Operational Responsibilities**

* **Fiduciary Validation & Payouts:**
  * Managing the full cycle of payment execution (Wise, PayPal, Bank Transfers).
  * Systematic verification of every invoice/receipt against Belgian "Public Utility Foundation" standards. He is the last line of defense against non-compliant spending.
* **Contract drafting**
  * Responsible for preparing all necessary contracts and agreements (Mandate Agreements, Service Contracts) based on the Foundation’s approved templates.
* **Accounting Coordination & Reporting:**
  * Liaison with Accountant & CFO: Primary point of contact for daily accounting queries. He ensures that the accountant has all necessary documents and explanations for month-end/year-end closing.
  * Audit Readiness: Maintaining an "audit-ready" digital archive of all justifications.
  * Operational Budget Encoding: Ensuring the Foundation's own operational costs are accurately tracked and reported to the Director and CFO.
* **Platform Expertise & Data Stewardship:**
  * Ensuring the "Source of Truth" (Open Collective) matches the bank reality through rigorous reconciliation.
  * Managing manual fund assignments and technical troubleshooting for the community.
* **Compliance Vetting (Tier 1):**
  * Onboarding standard Collectives and ensuring their setup strictly follows the "Indirect Representation" model.

**3. Operating Scope & Professional Boundaries**

* The Compliance Shield: Ivan has the mandate to block or pause any payment that does not meet the required documentation standards, protecting the Foundation’s legal status.
* Accounting Rigor: He focuses on *financial truth* and *process compliance*. He does not engage in strategic project negotiation or fee exceptions (which are handled by the Grant & Operations Lead).
* Escalation: Any complex grant structure (Tier 2/3) or non-standard financial flow must be transferred to Laura for operational design.

{% hint style="warning" %}
**Expected Outcomes**

* **Flawless Audits:** No missing receipts or non-compliant payments.
* **Operational Stability:** A platform that is always up-to-date and technical issues resolved swiftly.
* **Reliability:** Being the rock-solid foundation upon which the scaling of the Foundation is built.
  {% endhint %}


# Grant & Operations Lead (Laura)

{% hint style="info" %}
Current Position: Grant & Operations Lead

Reporting Line: Reports to the Executive Director
{% endhint %}

#### 1. Core Mission

The Grant & Operations Lead is the Director’s primary deputy. The role is designed to drive the Foundation’s scaling by professionalizing grant management, optimizing internal workflows, and ensuring high-level coordination of strategic funds. Laura acts as a bridge between vision and execution.

***

**2. Core Responsibilities & Scaling Pillars**

* **Strategic Grant Management (The GSF & Beyond):**&#x20;
  * Lead coordinator for Internal Funds and Tier 3 projects (e.g., Global Statelessness Fund).
  * Design and implement scalable grant-making workflows that can be replicated for future funds.
* **Contractual Oversight & Execution:**&#x20;
  * Responsible for the final review of all contracts drafted by Ivan. She ensures they align with the project’s specific needs and the Foundation’s strategy. She is the sole person responsible for sending contracts for signature via YouSign.
* **Operational Scaling & Process Design:**
  * Identify bottlenecks in the current hosting model and propose structural improvements.
  * Draft and update Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) to ensure the Foundation can handle a growing number of Collectives without losing quality.
* **Institutional Interface:**
  * Support the Director in high-level meetings with foundations and institutional partners.
  * Oversee complex compliance requirements (NGOsource, Equivalency Determinations) to position OCE as a top-tier European fiscal host.
* **Onboarding & Mandate Authority:**
  * Manage the legal and operational "architecture" of new complex projects.
  * Ensure all "Mandate Agreements" for strategic initiatives are bulletproof and aligned with the Foundation's public interest mission.

{% hint style="warning" %}

#### Boundaries & Escalation (Strict)

* **Supervision of Back-Office Flow:** While Ivan executes the payments, Laura defines the operational standards and priorities he must follow. She ensures the "Back-Office" scales at the same pace as the "Front-Office."
* **Strict Fiduciary Alignment:** As a "Sparring Partner," she must challenge any project or expenditure that risks the Foundation’s "Public Utility" status or deviates from the "Indirect Representation" model.
* **No Tax/Legal Advice:** Strictly adheres to the rule of directing members to qualified experts for personal tax/VAT matters.
  {% endhint %}


# Tools & Platforms Used at OCE

Core tools and platforms used by the Open Collective Europe Foundation

### Open Collective Platform

[**opencollective.com**](https://opencollective.com)

The central tool used to manage all collectives, fund balances, expenses, updates, public transparency, and donations.

We use:

* **Admin view** to monitor activity, flag irregularities, and update collective settings
* **Expense review features** to approve and reject payouts
* **Reports & CSV exports** for reconciliation and grant reporting

Note: As the Foundation is a fiscal host on the platform, all hosted collectives are linked under [opencollective.com/europe](https://opencollective.com/europe)

***

### GitBook

[**docs.opencollective.com/oceurope**](https://docs.opencollective.com/oceurope)

Used to manage external and internal documentation (this handbook), external-facing support pages, onboarding guides, and legal references.\
GitBook allows collaborative editing, version control, and publishing workflows.

***

### Slack

We use the shared **Open Collective Slack** workspace.

* The `#oce-internal` channel is our main internal thread
* We also use `#support`, `#expenses`, and `#tax-deductibility` for coordination across entities
* Direct messages and ad-hoc groups for issue triage

If you’re new, ask to be invited via an existing team member or <oce@opencollective.com>

***

### Google Workspace

* **Drive**: Shared folders for legal docs, onboarding materials, grant agreements, and working files
* **Gmail**: Team aliases (e.g. oce\@...) and personal addresses
* **Docs/Sheets**: For working documents, checklists, and spreadsheets

Main folders:

* `OCE - Admin`
* `OCE - Hosted Collectives`
* `OCE - Funders`
* `OCE - Legal & Compliance`

***

### Freshdesk

[**support.opencollective.com**](https://support.opencollective.com)

Used to respond to incoming questions from hosted collectives or donors.\
We mostly handle Tier 2 tickets, including:

* Complex expense issues
* US/Europe tax questions
* Manual bank transfers
* Custom grant workflows

Freshdesk is managed in collaboration with OC Inc. support team.

***

### Discord

Used occasionally for ecosystem-wide calls or developer coordination.\
Not a primary support or communication channel for OCE.

***

### Internal Reminders

* Whenever possible, centralize documentation in GitBook
* Keep Drive folders organized and searchable by others
* Use platform-native tools (e.g. Open Collective admin features) before duplicating info elsewhere
* Tag teammates on Slack for issues requiring action

***

Let the team know if you need access to any of the tools above.


# AI Usage Policy

### 1. Purpose

This policy defines how AI tools can be used within Open Collective Europe to support:

* email communication
* operational tasks
* strategic thinking
* documentation

The objective is to:

* improve clarity and efficiency
* reduce errors
* support better decision-making

{% hint style="warning" %}
**AI is a support tool, not a decision-maker.**
{% endhint %}

***

### 2. General Principles

#### 2.1 Human Responsibility

All outputs generated using AI remain the responsibility of the person using it.

* AI suggestions must always be reviewed
* No message or decision should be sent blindly

{% hint style="warning" %}
**AI assists. Humans decide.**
{% endhint %}

***

#### 2.2 Accuracy & Verification

AI can:

* be incomplete
* be incorrect
* misunderstand context

Therefore:

* all factual, financial, or operational information must be verified
* AI should not be relied on for precise data without validation

***

#### 2.3 Context Awareness

AI does not know:

* internal agreements
* specific collective situations
* historical context

Users must:

* provide sufficient context when using AI
* ensure outputs align with actual situations

***

### 3. Mandatory Use: AI as a Sparring Partner

For any:

* strategic thinking
* important decisions
* complex situations
* sensitive communications

AI must be used as an intellectual sparring partner, not just as a writing tool.

#### Required Prompt

{% hint style="warning" %}
**The following prompt must be used when engaging AI in this context:**
{% endhint %}

> **Intellectual Sparring Prompt**
>
> From now on, do not simply affirm my statements or assume my conclusions are correct. Your goal is to be an intellectual sparring partner, not just an agreeable assistant.<br>
>
> Every time I present an idea, do the following:
>
> 1. Analyze my assumptions. What am I taking for granted that might not be true?
>
> 2. Provide counterpoints. What would a smart, well-informed skeptic say in response?
>
> 3. Test my reasoning. Does my logic hold up under scrutiny, or are there gaps or flaws I haven’t considered?
>
> 4. Offer alternative perspectives. How else could this idea be framed, interpreted, or challenged?
>
> 5. Prioritize truth over agreement. If I am wrong or my reasoning is weak, I need to know. Correct me clearly and explain why.
>
> Maintain a constructive but rigorous approach. Your role is not to argue for the sake of arguing, but to push me toward greater clarity, precision, and intellectual honesty.<br>
>
> If I start falling into confirmation bias or making unchecked assumptions, call it out directly. Let’s refine not only our conclusions, but also how we arrive at them.

#### Objective

* improve reasoning quality
* challenge assumptions
* avoid confirmation bias
* increase clarity and rigor

***

### 4. Approved Use Cases

#### 4.1 Email Drafting & Review

AI can be used to:

* draft replies
* improve tone and clarity
* structure complex explanations

Best practices:

* provide context
* review tone carefully
* adapt output to the recipient

***

#### 4.2 External Communication Review

Before sending important messages:

* AI should be used to check:
  * tone
  * clarity
  * helpfulness
  * potential friction

***

#### 4.3 Internal Documentation

AI can be used to:

* structure documents
* improve clarity
* rephrase content

All content must be reviewed and validated.

***

#### 4.4 Strategic Thinking

AI must be used as a sparring partner for:

* strategy
* positioning
* decision-making

***

#### 4.5 Operational Support

AI can assist with:

* process clarification
* drafting internal notes
* structuring workflows

But:

* it does not replace operational verification

***

### 5. Recommended Tools

#### 5.1 Gemini (Google Workspace)

Open Collective Europe has access to Gemini (Pro) via Google Workspace.<br>

Recommended usage:

* create a dedicated Gem for:
  * operations
  * communication review
  * strategic thinking
* connect the Gem to the relevant Google Drive folder

  <https://drive.google.com/drive/u/1/folders/13NbaEfARaoes-pxs57FeoQVggaSQHdrp>

This allows:

* better contextual responses
* more relevant outputs
* consistency across usage

***

### 6. Restricted Uses

AI must not be used to:

* make financial decisions
* validate payments or compliance
* provide final answers on legal or accounting matters
* communicate externally without review

***

### 7. Communication Guidelines

<br>

When using AI for external communication:

* messages must remain:
  * human
  * clear
  * aligned with our tone
* avoid:
  * generic or robotic language
  * overly formal tone
* always adapt:
  * to the recipient
  * to the context

***

### 8. Data & Confidentiality

* Avoid sharing highly sensitive or confidential information
* Be cautious with:
  * financial data
  * personal data
  * partner-specific agreements

<br>

When in doubt:

* reduce the level of detail

***

### 9. Expected Behavior

<br>

AI should be used to:

* improve quality
* reduce errors
* challenge thinking
* support autonomy

<br>

Not to:

* shortcut reasoning
* avoid responsibility
* bypass verification

***

### 10. Key Principle

<br>

> AI is a tool to improve both execution and thinking — not to replace judgment.


# Key Links & Meeting Rhythms

This page centralizes key links and the recurring internal routines that structure collaboration within Open Collective Europe Foundation.

### Shared Drive folders

* **Admin & Legal**\
  [Drive – OCE Admin](https://drive.google.com/drive/folder/...)\
  Governance, incorporation, and compliance documents.
* **Hosted Collectives**\
  [Drive – OCE Hosted Collectives](https://drive.google.com/drive/folder/...)\
  Subfolders for each hosted initiative, grant documents, and notes.
* **Templates & Docs**\
  [Drive – OCE Templates](https://drive.google.com/drive/folder/...)\
  Expense guidelines, onboarding kits, response drafts.

{% hint style="info" %}
Make sure to request access to these folders if you don’t already have it.
{% endhint %}

***

### Internal communication

* **Email**: <oce@opencollective.com>
* **Slack channel**: `#ocef`
* **Freshdesk**: [support.opencollective.com](https://support.opencollective.com)

***

### Weekly schedule

* **Monday**
  * Financial review and synchronization of contributions transferred to collectives by manual bank transfer.
  * Bank sync through the platform using **Ledger → Bank Sync**.
  * Review of pending emails and Freshdesk tickets.
  * Slack check-in at **11:00 CET**.
  * Global OFiCo Team call at **15:00 CET**.
  * Onboarding calls with collectives, when scheduled.
  * Estimate available funds and expected payout capacity for the week or upcoming period.
* **Tuesday**
  * Expenses payout day.
  * Slack check-in at **11:00 CET**.
  * Expense processing order:
    1. OCEF USD expenses
    2. OCEF EUR expenses
    3. OSE expenses
  * After payouts are processed, perform a second control round to check whether submitters have responded to incomplete expense requests.
  * Freshdesk follow-up for finance-related tickets.
* **Wednesday**
  * Ad hoc check-ins or funder calls
  * Evaluation of new applications.
  * Exchanges with collectives regarding onboarding, eligibility, missing information, or next steps.
  * Collective onboarding, when scheduled.
  * Freshdesk review.
* **Thursday**
  * Expenses payout day.
  * Slack check-in at **11:00 CET**.
  * Internal backlog check-in.
  * EU Fiscal Hosts call, once per month.
  * Collective onboarding, when scheduled.
  * Follow-up on expenses, pending payout issues, and operational blockers.
* **Friday**
  * No official meetings (quiet work time)
  * Freshdesk and email follow-up.
  * Reconciliation of transactions in Zoho/Odoo.
  * Catch-up on the expense processing backlog.
  * Review and close remaining operational or finance follow-ups before the weekend.

***

### Suggestions

If you're onboarding or unsure where to find something:

* Ask in `#ocef` Slack
* Check GitBook first
* Flag outdated links or documents in Drive to be cleaned up


# Email Filters – Suggested Setup

This page outlines useful Gmail filters to keep OCE-related communications organized and searchable. These are not mandatory, but they help reduce noise and ensure that important messages aren’t missed.

***

If you're added as an admin to the Open Collective Europe's [Collective](https://opencollective.com/opensource), you're going to start receiving a ton of emails. This is because every collective that is fiscally hosted by OCE will send every email notification to you, too.This is not ideal.In order to deal with these emails, we suggest you use your work email for your Open Collective account. Then, set up these filters in Gmail. These are from [/u/rPsmuFj6T1dVqQul5FdOSLdamSI2](http:///u/rPsmuFj6T1dVqQul5FdOSLdamSI2), and may need to be modified for you.

Apply labels:

* Matches: to:(\*@opencollective.com) Do this: Apply label "Open Collective"
* Matches: from:(no-reply@\*.opencollective.com) Do this: Apply label "Open Collective"
* Matches: from:(<notification@slack.com>) opencollective.slack.com Do this: Apply label "Open Collective"

Skip inbox:

* Matches: from:(<no-reply@opencollective.com>) Do this: Skip Inbox, Apply label "no-reply\@oc"
* Matches: from:(<info@opencollective.com>) Do this: Skip Inbox

Delete:

* Matches: from:(<info@opencollective.com>) subject:(Has $ arrived) Do this: Delete it
* Matches: from:(<info@opencollective.com>) subject:(\* "wants to be hosted by Open Source Collective") Do this: Delete it
* Matches: from:(<info@opencollective.com>) subject:("New pending financial contribution") Do this: Delete it

***

### General recommendations

* Use color labels to distinguish automated vs. action-required emails
* Consider bundling low-priority labels into the Gmail Promotions/Updates tab
* Star messages that require a follow-up within the week
* Revisit filters monthly to adapt as your role evolves

***

Need help configuring filters?\
Ask someone in the team or check the Gmail filter guide [here](https://support.google.com/mail/answer/6579?hl=en).


# Due Diligence Procedures

This page summarizes the internal due diligence process we follow at Open Collective Europe Foundation before accepting a new initiative or approving certain types of funding.

The objective is to ensure that each hosted initiative aligns with our public interest mission, complies with applicable legal frameworks, and presents a manageable level of financial and reputational risk.

***

### Types of due diligence

We distinguish between two main levels of review:

#### 1. Standard due diligence

Applies to most initiatives hosted under the Foundation.

We verify:

* Legal status (if any)
* Public communications (website, social media, etc.)
* Mission alignment
* Geographic focus
* Team transparency
* Expected budget and income sources

This review is typically handled by the executive director, with support from ops if needed.

***

#### 2. Enhanced due diligence

Triggered when:

* The initiative is politically sensitive
* The country of operation appears on international risk or sanctions lists
* The initiative receives large grants or public subsidies
* The funder requests extra compliance documentation
* We have internal flags based on past issues or external input

Additional steps may include:

* Reviewing formal incorporation documents
* Checking funders and beneficiaries
* Requesting signed declarations
* Involving legal counsel for analysis

***

### Risk flags to monitor

* Ambiguous or inconsistent public presence
* Excessive opacity around team structure or financial flows
* Aggressive or politically extreme messaging
* Use of external bank accounts (not allowed)
* Refusal to provide basic documentation

***

### Documentation checklist

For enhanced cases or grant-funded projects, we may request:

* Copy of official ID from admins
* Signed statement of purpose or intent
* Proof of registration (if incorporated)
* Funding proposal or budget plan
* Grant agreement or funding terms (if applicable)

All documents are stored in the relevant Drive folders, labeled clearly with the Collective name.

***

### Decision-making and recordkeeping

Final decisions on onboarding or escalation are made by the executive director, in consultation with the financial operations team and legal advisors if needed.

Notes should be kept in:

* GitBook (DD summary, if applicable)
* Drive (raw documents and review sheets)
* The Collective’s admin notes field on the platform (if relevant)

***

For questions or to report concerns related to a new application, contact the executive director directly or raise the issue in the `#ocef` Slack channel.


# Due Diligence for Unregistered Groups

This page outlines the specific due diligence checks we apply when reviewing collectives or initiatives that are **not incorporated** (i.e., no legal entity).

These groups make up a large part of our hosted ecosystem and are particularly important for our mission. However, they require adapted review procedures to ensure compliance and mitigate risk.

***

### What we check

For unregistered initiatives, we focus on:

#### 1. Team transparency

* Full names of at least two core team members
* Public presence of these individuals (on the Collective page or linked sites)
* Admins must not use pseudonyms or nicknames exclusively

> Note: If a collective insists on full anonymity, escalate the case to the executive director for review.

***

#### 2. Mission and activities

* Clear articulation of purpose and intended public benefit
* Descriptions on the platform, website, or social media
* No evidence of political campaigning, for-profit motives, or high-risk activities

***

#### 3. Geographic indicators

* Country or region of operation must be declared
* If operating from a high-risk or sanctioned country, trigger enhanced due diligence
* Currency of use (EUR, USD, crypto?) may also guide review

***

#### 4. Funding intent

* Is the initiative likely to seek foundation grants or public funding?
  * If yes: advise incorporation or consider hosting under the ASBL instead
* If it's mostly community donations: can remain hosted under the Foundation with standard controls

***

### Additional measures

In higher-risk cases or when doubts persist, we may request:

* A short project description in writing
* A signed declaration of purpose
* Screenshots or links showing public-facing activity
* A brief onboarding call with the team

All additional documentation is to be stored in the relevant folder in Drive, and flagged in the internal notes of the platform profile.

***

### When to escalate

Escalate to the executive director if:

* The team is fully anonymous
* The project operates in a conflict zone or sanctioned jurisdiction
* Large funders are involved with limited transparency
* Any form of public disinformation, hate speech, or extremism is detected

***

### Hosting structure recommendation

* If the initiative is small, informal, and community-based → host under the **Foundation**
* If the initiative is structurally unclear and may evolve → consider **ASBL**
* If they expect to receive major grants → advise formal incorporation or use a third-party fiscal host

***

Questions about onboarding an unregistered group?\
Raise it in the `#oce-internal` Slack or contact the executive director.


# Risk and Compliance

This page outlines the principles and criteria we apply internally to evaluate risk and ensure compliance with legal obligations, funder expectations, and OCE’s values.

These guidelines are used to:

* Vet incoming applications
* Respond to due diligence queries from funders
* Escalate edge cases that may require legal or governance intervention

***

### Core Principles

We aim to support grassroots, mission-driven initiatives — including those working in politically or socially complex environments — while remaining fully compliant with:

* Belgian nonprofit law
* Anti-money laundering (AML) regulations
* International standards on counter-terrorism financing
* Funders’ due diligence frameworks

***

### Compliance risk indicators

The following are considered **potential red flags** in an application or during hosting:

* Incomplete or pseudonymous team profiles
* No public-facing presence (website, social media, platform activity)
* Use of external/private bank accounts
* Politically extreme messaging or opaque networks
* Funders, beneficiaries, or team members based in high-risk jurisdictions (e.g. under EU or UN sanctions)

***

### Information sources we use

* Collective’s public profile and communications
* Project websites and social media
* News reports and independent media
* International NGO databases
* Sanctions lists (EU, UN, OFAC if applicable)

***

### How we assess alignment

We evaluate whether the initiative:

* Has a clear and legitimate public interest purpose
* Aligns with OCE’s impact areas and grantmaking goals
* Operates with transparency and accountability
* Avoids any form of discrimination, hate speech, or disinformation

***

### Escalation process

Cases are escalated to the executive director when:

* A collective raises significant legal or reputational risk
* We are contacted by a funder regarding concerns
* Conflicting information emerges after onboarding

Escalated cases may result in:

* Refusal of application
* Request for additional documentation
* Hosting under ASBL instead of Foundation
* Legal consultation
* Termination of hosting

***

### Mitigation practices

For sensitive or higher-risk collectives, we may:

* Limit payout methods (e.g. no crypto)
* Require stricter documentation (e.g. ID verification)
* Restrict use of certain tier types
* Review public updates periodically

***

### Decision-making

Final decisions related to onboarding or termination are made by the executive director in consultation with financial ops and, where necessary, legal counsel.

All decisions must be documented:

* In the collective’s internal notes on the platform
* In the GitBook risk log (if applicable)
* In Drive, within the due diligence folder for the project


# Financial and Legal Operational Procedures

This page provides an internal reference for how Open Collective Europe Foundation manages key legal and financial processes. These procedures are adapted to our legal structure as a Belgian public utility foundation, and the operational requirements of fiscal hosting.

***

### Grant acceptance and fund reception

* All grant agreements must be signed by an OCE administrator (Jean-François, Shannon, or Pia).
* A service agreement is required between the hosted initiative and OCE before accepting the grant.
* OCE receives the funds into its central bank account and allocates them to the corresponding Collective via the platform.
* The agreement and documentation are stored in the relevant funder and Collective folders in Drive.

***

### Tax-deductible donations (US)

* We process tax-deductible donations for U.S.-based donors through Myriad USA.
* Donors can route contributions through Myriad’s interface, which generates a tax receipt.
* These funds are later transferred to OCE and allocated to the intended Collective.
* All Myriad-related donations should be clearly marked in Drive and tracked in platform Notes.

***

### Bank transfers (incoming)

* Incoming transfers are monitored twice weekly (Monday and Thursday).
* Ivan allocates untagged transfers based on order numbers, donor names, or email confirmation.
* If no identification is possible, the funds are held in a temporary holding Collective.
* At 2 years unclaimed, they are transferred to the Foundation’s general reserves.

***

### Expense processing

* Collectives submit expenses via the platform (invoice or reimbursement).
* Two layers of approval:
  1. Collective admin
  2. OCE financial operations (Ivan)
* Payouts are processed via Wise or PayPal every Monday and Thursday.
* All expenses must have appropriate documentation. No pro forma invoices or order confirmations are accepted.
* Crypto expenses are not supported.

***

### Risk flagging and documentation

* Any flagged project (due to due diligence, grant terms, political context, etc.) must be marked clearly:
  * In the platform internal notes
  * In GitBook (Risk Log or escalation page)
  * In the Collective’s Drive folder
* When in doubt, escalate to the executive director.

***

### Legal document management

* All signed contracts, funding agreements, and due diligence files must be stored in Google Drive.
* Folder naming format: `YYYY-MM_ProjectName_DocumentType` (e.g. `2024-03_SolidarityKitchen_GrantAgreement.pdf`)
* No legal documents should be stored only in email or Slack.

***

### Compliance and audits

* OCE must be ready to respond to funder audits or legal inquiries at all times.
* All funder documentation (application, budget, signed agreement, reports) should be centralized in Drive and referenced in GitBook if useful.
* The executive director is the primary point of contact for compliance matters.

***

If you’re unsure where or how to process a legal or financial action, ask in `#ocef` or contact Jean-François or Ivan directly.


# Handling Non-Registered Organizations

This page outlines internal procedures for onboarding and managing collectives that are **not legally registered** (i.e., they do not have NGO or nonprofit status in their jurisdiction).

These initiatives are common and often well-aligned with OCE’s mission, but they require extra attention for legal, compliance, and communications purposes.

***

### Key criteria

Unregistered groups must meet the following conditions to be hosted under the Foundation:

* A clearly stated mission in the public interest
* At least two identifiable administrators
* A verifiable public presence (website, social media, publications, etc.)
* Transparent communication about their team and activities
* A relatively low funding volume, or clear funding sources (if larger)

If any of these criteria are not met, pause onboarding and escalate for review.

***

### Review process

1. **Initial application**\
   Collect information on mission, team, geography, and expected funding.
2. **Visibility & legitimacy check**
   * Does the initiative have a consistent, public presence?
   * Are the admins known or accountable individuals?
3. **Internal flagging (if needed)**\
   Flag in Slack or GitBook if the group:
   * Is anonymous
   * Is politically sensitive
   * Operates in a high-risk region
4. **Decision on structure**
   * **Use the Foundation** for small or moderately visible projects
   * **Use the ASBL** for experimental or local initiatives without external funding
   * **Recommend incorporation** if the group plans to receive large grants or ongoing public subsidies

***

### Hosting precautions

For unregistered groups:

* Do not allow use of the term “charity” or “nonprofit organization” in descriptions unless appropriate in their country
* Ensure all platform descriptions clearly state the initiative is hosted by OCE
* Add an internal note explaining the group’s status (unregistered) and why it was accepted
* Consider requiring a brief mission declaration and public-facing contact person

***

### Donor communication

* US-based donors: Explain that tax-deductible donations are possible via Myriad USA, but only for eligible groups
* Institutional funders: If they require incorporation or 501(c)(3) equivalency, OCE may not be a suitable host for the group

***

### Long-term recommendation

We encourage collectives to consider legal registration if they plan to:

* Apply for grants regularly
* Hire staff
* Partner with public institutions
* Build significant annual budgets

We can advise on transitions and potential partnerships with other fiscal hosts or intermediaries.


# Creation and Sending of Mandate Agreements

#### Purpose and scope

To ensure that mandate agreements are drafted, reviewed, signed, stored, and uploaded consistently and in line with OCEF requirements.\
This procedure applies to all mandate agreements.

#### **Introduction**

Mandate agreements define the relationship between OCE and the initiatives in cases where a formal document is needed to evidence the nature of the hosting arrangement to a third party, such as a grant maker. They clarify the respective roles and responsibilities of OCE and the hosted initiative in relation to a specific activity or arrangement. Mandate agreements are mandatory in cases involving the reception of a grant. Where a collective is only carrying out simple fundraising activities, the Terms of Hosting are sufficient and no mandate agreement is required.

#### Responsibilities

The Director initiates the process and indicates which template should be used.

Operations prepares the draft, performs the required checks, sends the agreement for signature, and ensures proper storage and upload.

Special cases may be identified and handled by either Operations or the Director, depending on the circumstances.

1. Process initiation\
   \
   The Director requests the preparation of a mandate agreement.\
   Operations can propose a creation of a mandate agreement when deemed necessary.<br>

2. Template selection\
   \
   Agreement templates are prepared by the legal team (external lawyers) and stored on Google Drive - [OCEF](https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1Zs26YrAQ5vav4btWBCuLLb2RMcK3orAE?usp=drive_link) and [OSE](https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1_3wi8ooSbW4tlwynkOIWdZ8QZnDGYoG8?usp=drive_link).\
   Operations must use the template indicated by the Director.<br>

3. Drafting the agreement\
   \
   Operations completes the selected template with the relevant information. At least two administrators of the initiative must be included, with their legal names, addresses, email addresses, and, where required, ID numbers.<br>

4. Internal review\
   \
   Operations reviews the draft and raises any uncertainty internally or with the Director, as appropriate.<br>

5. Sanctions check\
   \
   Before sending the agreement, Operations must check the relevant parties against sanctions lists:
   1. [OpenSanctions](https://www.opensanctions.org/)
   2. if needed, [EU Sanctions Map<br>](https://www.sanctionsmap.eu/#/main)

6. Key drafting checks\
   \
   The agreement must:&#x20;
   1. remain narrow and task-specific;
   2. keep OCEF (OSE) in an intermediary or indirect representation role;
   3. keep the initiative responsible for its own activities;
   4. include all relevant administrators as signatories;
   5. provide OCEF (OSE) with broad authority to perform the agreed service;
   6. preserve indemnity, withholding, and deduction rights;
   7. avoid wording that would make OCEF (OSE) the general operator, owner, or tax-responsible party beyond the specific arrangement documented.

7. Signature process\
   \
   The draft is sent for signature through [Yousign](https://yousign.app/auth/workspace/requests).\
   Operations must include the signature field and the mention Operations must include the signature field and the mention “signed on date.”<br>

8. Storage and upload\
   \
   After signature, the agreement must be stored on Google Drive in the collective’s folder. It must also be uploaded under Dashboard > Agreements, assigned to the relevant collective, and saved without an end date.

9. Special cases

Any special case identified during the process should be reviewed internally and, where appropriate, jointly by Operations and the Director before the agreement is finalized.

<br>


# Brand

Our brand is currently being redesigned


# Grantmaking Procedures

This page outlines how Open Collective Europe Foundation manages incoming grants, outgoing grants (regranting), and related documentation and compliance processes.

***

### Incoming grants to OCE

OCE can receive grants from:

* Private foundations (including U.S.-based)
* Public funders (municipal, national, EU) - To be discussed with the team
* Other nonprofits or networks

#### Key requirements

* The grant agreement must be signed by an authorized board member (Jean-François, Pia, or Shannon)
* A **service agreement** must be signed between OCE and the receiving collective or project
* All grant-related documents (contract, budget, communication, deliverables) must be stored in the relevant Drive folder

{% hint style="info" %}
A due diligence check is performed before any grant is accepted
{% endhint %}

***

### Regranting through OCE

OCE can act as an intermediary to **distribute funds to multiple collectives or projects**, under a single donor grant. These are internally referred to as **Funds**.

#### Conditions for managing a Fund:

* There must be at least two fund administrators
* Selection of subgrantees must follow a transparent and documented process
* OCE must retain oversight over the fund's alignment with its public benefit mission
* Grant agreements or distribution criteria must be documented in Drive

Examples include:

* Hosting a fund on behalf of a foundation for a thematic call
* Running a regranting campaign for a network or coalition

{% hint style="info" %}
Funds are always hosted under the Foundation, never under the ASBL.
{% endhint %}

***

### Compliance and risk

For all grants:

* Funds must be used in accordance with the stated purpose
* Regranting decisions must be defensible in case of audit or public review
* No funds may be redistributed to for-profit entities, unless properly invoiced and justified
* Final beneficiaries should align with OCE's mission and be traceable

For U.S. funders:

* Use Myriad USA to ensure tax-deductible compliance when needed
* If equivalency determination is not possible, consult with OCE admin team for alternative routing

***

### Reporting to funders

OCE supports both the administrative and content side of reporting, depending on the agreement.

Typical reports include:

* Financial statements (downloadable from the platform)
* Narrative updates (published on the collective or fund page)
* Optional impact reports (shared via PDF or Google Docs)

All reporting deadlines and requirements must be tracked in the collective or fund's Drive folder and Slack notes.

***

### Internal tracking

Each grant should be documented in:

* Google Drive: all agreements and correspondence
* GitBook (optional): if relevant for ongoing documentation
* Platform: notes field or Fund profile for reference

When in doubt about grant acceptance or distribution, escalate to the executive director.


# Receiving Funds from the U.S.

This page outlines how the Open Collective Europe Foundation (OCE) receives and processes funds from U.S.-based donors, including individual donors and private foundations.

OCE is not a U.S. legal entity, but we have structured mechanisms to legally and transparently receive U.S. funds.

{% hint style="danger" %}
**Important**: These services are available **only to collectives hosted by the Foundation,** not by the ASBL. &#x20;

If a collective hosted under the ASBL wishes to access U.S. fundraising mechanisms, it must first be transferred to the Foundation. Contact the team to initiate this process.
{% endhint %}

***

### 1. Tax-deductible donations from individual donors

When a hosted collective wants to receive tax-deductible donations from individual donors in the United States, the process is as follows:

#### Step-by-step process:

1. **Collective request**\
   The collective contacts OCE to request support for receiving U.S. tax-deductible donations.
2. **Setup via Every.org**\
   We provide the collective with a link and instructions to set up a fundraising page on [Every.org](https://www.every.org/), a U.S.-based donation platform.
3. **Donations via Every.org**\
   U.S. donors contribute through Every.org and receive a tax-deductible receipt.\
   The collective does not interact with Myriad USA (Every.org's fiscal sponsor).
4. **End of campaign**\
   When the collective informs us that they are done fundraising via Every.org, we initiate the next step.
5. **Fund transfer from Myriad USA**\
   OCE contacts Myriad USA (the fiscal sponsor holding the funds) and requests the transfer of the collected donations. This is treated as a grant from Myriad USA to the Foundation.
6. **Funds are received and credited**\
   Once OCE receives the funds from Myriad USA, we allocate them to the relevant collective on the Open Collective platform.

#### Fees:

* **OCE applies its standard 10% hosting fee** on all funds received.
* **Myriad USA also deducts a fee** (typically between 1–2%) before sending the grant to OCE.&#x20;
* Collectives should be informed that **only the OCE fee (10%) applies to their received amount**, regardless of Myriad's internal handling fee.

***

### 2. Grants from U.S.-based private foundations

OCE is eligible to directly receive grants from U.S.-based foundations thanks to its **Equivalency Determination Certificate (EDC)**, which establishes it as the equivalent of a U.S. 501(c)(3) public charity.

#### Step-by-step process:

1. **Service agreement**\
   Before any grant can be accepted, a **service agreement** must be signed between OCE and the collective.
2. **Grant approval**\
   The collective leads the grant application process with the U.S. foundation.\
   Once approved, OCE steps in as the legal recipient.
3. **Direct funder coordination**\
   OCE signs the **grant agreement**, provides its **banking information**, and shares the **Equivalency Determination Certificate** with the funder.
4. **Fund reception and credit**\
   Upon receipt of the grant, OCE allocates the amount to the collective’s balance on the platform.

#### Fees:

* OCE applies its **standard 10% fee** on all grants received from U.S.-based foundations.
* No additional external fee (e.g., Myriad) applies in this scenario.

***

### 3. Direct (non-deductible) donations

If a donor — individual or organization — **does not require a tax receipt**, they can give directly via:

* Credit or debit card on the platform
* PayPal
* Manual bank transfer to OCE (with correct reference)

These donations are simpler and faster to process, and still subject to the 10% OCE hosting fee.

***

### Internal tracking and compliance

* **All grants and major donations** must be backed by a signed service agreement.
* **All documents** (service agreements, grant agreements, funder communications) should be stored in the collective’s Drive folder.
* Use internal notes on the platform and GitBook to flag special handling cases (e.g., Myriad, ED Certificate use).
* For anything unclear, escalate to the executive director or financial operations team.

***

Questions? Contact <oce@opencollective.com>


# Myriad USA

## Tax-Deductible Donations from the U.S. (Every.org / Myriad USA)

This page explains how U.S.-based donors can make tax-deductible donations to collectives hosted by **Open Collective Europe Foundation**, through **Myriad USA**.

There are **two different routes**, depending on the type of donation:

* **Every.org** is used for **public fundraising, crowdfunding, or repeated donations from many small U.S. donors**
* **Direct coordination with OCE / Myriad USA** is used for **large one-off donations that will not go through a public fundraising page**, typically by wire transfer

{% hint style="info" %}
This process is part of the same mechanism described in the "Receiving Funds from the U.S." page.\
Every.org provides the public-facing donation page, while Myriad USA serves as the fiscal sponsor and fund holder.
{% endhint %}

***

### Who is this for?

This route is available for:

* **U.S.-based individuals or companies** seeking a U.S. tax-deductible receipt
* **Collectives hosted by Open Collective Europe Foundation**

{% hint style="danger" %}
This route is **not** intended for U.S. foundations making grants. Foundations should instead use the **Equivalency Determination Certificate (EDC)** process.
{% endhint %}

***

### Which route should be used?

#### 1. Every.org: crowdfunding and repeated small donations

This route should be used when a collective wants to raise funds from many U.S.-based donors, especially through a public campaign, crowdfunding, or repeated small donations.

In this case:

* OCE sends the collective a link to create a fundraising page on Every.org
* Donors give through the public page
* Myriad USA acts as the U.S. fiscal sponsor and legal recipient of the funds
* Donors receive their tax-deductible receipt from Myriad USA
* No separate coordination with Myriad USA is normally needed for standard donations made through Every.org

#### 2. Direct coordination: large donations outside crowdfunding

This route should be used when a major donor wants to make a substantial one-off donation and will not donate through a public crowdfunding page.

In this case:

* the collective can share the donor’s details with OCE, or
* the donor can contact OCE directly

OCE then shares the relevant donation instructions for Myriad USA.

If applicable, the donation may be completed by wire transfer.

The donor receives the tax-deductible receipt from Myriad USA.

### How it works

#### Option 1 — Every.org for crowdfunding and repeated donations

**Request from the collective**

The collective informs OCE that it wants to receive U.S.-based, tax-deductible donations from individuals or companies through a public fundraising page.

**Page creation on Every.org**

OCE sends the collective a link to create a fundraising page on Every.org.

Every.org is connected to Myriad USA, which acts as the fiscal sponsor and legal recipient of the funds in the U.S.

**Donations and tax receipts**

Donors contribute through the collective’s public Every.org page.

They receive a tax-deductible receipt from Myriad USA (501(c)(3)).

The collective does not need to coordinate separately with Myriad USA for standard Every.org donations.

#### **Option 2 — Large donation outside Every.org**

1. **Initial coordination**

   If a donor wishes to make a **large donation outside the public fundraising page**, the process is handled separately.

   Either:

   * the collective sends OCE the donor’s details, or
   * the donor contacts OCE directly

   The information shared should include, where relevant:

   * donor’s full name
   * donor’s contact details
   * expected donation amount
   * intended collective / project
   * whether a company matching program is involved (i.e. Benevity or Cybergrants).
   * We then pass along this information to Suzana Erickson, the Myriad USA responsible for Europe - <suzana@myriadusa.org>.

   **Donation instructions**

   OCE then shares the relevant **Myriad USA donation instructions** directly for that donation.

   Where needed, the donation can be made by wire transfer to Myriad USA.

   **Tax receipt**

   Once the donation is processed, Myriad USA issues the tax-deductible receipt to the donor.
2. **Fund release**\
   Once the collective informs OCE that they are done fundraising on Every.org, we follow this procedure:
   1. Go to <https://www.grantinterface.com/Dashboard/Applicant>
   2. Enter login details
   3. Click on Apply and enter the code `friends`
   4. submit the application (one application with several projects listed)
3. **Allocation**\
   Once OCE receives the funds, we credit them to the collective’s balance on the Open Collective platform.

***

### Fees

#### Myriad USA

Myriad USA charges a fee based on the total amount collected:

* **5%** on the first $100,000
* **3%** from $100,001 to $500,000
* **1%** from $500,001 to $1,000,000
* **0.5%** on any amount above $1,000,000
* An additional **3%** processing fee applies to **credit card donations**

#### Open Collective Europe

OCE applies its **standard 10% hosting fee** to all amounts received via this channel.

{% hint style="warning" %}
Myriad’s fee is deducted before the transfer to OCE. The OCE fee is calculated on the net amount received.
{% endhint %}

***

### Wire transfer instructions (if used)

If a donor prefers to contribute by wire, the following information applies:

**Bank**: JPMorgan Chase, 270 Park Avenue, New York, NY, 10017\
**Routing Number**: 021000021 (domestic)\
**SWIFT/BIC**: CHASUS33 (international)\
**Beneficiary**: MyriadUSA, 551 Fifth Avenue, Suite 2400, New York, NY 10176\
**Account Number**: 939282908

***

### Donor coordination

Donors who wire funds should contact directly OCE.

OCE then contacts:

**Suzana Erickson – Myriad USA**\
📧 <suzana@myriadusa.org>\
📞 (212) 713-7661

Include:

* Donor’s full name and contact details
* Date and amount of transfer
* Intended use (project name / Collective)
* Whether a company matching program is involved (i.e. Benevity or Cybergrants).

***

### End-of-year reporting

At the end of each year, OCE must submit a brief report on how the funds were used, based on updates received from the collective.\
This report is consolidated and sent to **Myriad USA**, as required for fiscal transparency.

***

### Notes

* This mechanism is only available to collectives hosted under the **Foundation** (not the ASBL)
* Use Every.org for public-facing fundraising; no separate application to Myriad is required
* All related documents must be saved in the collective’s Drive folder and flagged in internal notes


# Receiving Funds from Europe

This page summarizes the general approach for managing contributions and grants from philanthropic organizations or donors based in the European Union.

At present, there is no standardized or automated process, and each case is handled individually based on the requirements of the funder.

***

### General principles

* Open Collective Europe Foundation is legally based in Belgium and recognized as a **public utility foundation** (*fondation d’utilité publique*).
* This status is generally well received by EU-based foundations, but each institution has its own due diligence and contractual requirements.
* We typically position ourselves as the **fiscal host and legal recipient** of funds, and sign agreements directly with funders.

***

### Process (flexible case-by-case)

1. **The collective leads the conversation with the foundation**\
   In most cases, the initiative opens the discussion or applies for funding.
2. **OCE is looped in once the foundation requires a legal recipient**\
   At that point, we review the documentation and coordinate directly with the foundation’s grants or legal team.
3. **Agreement and reception**\
   OCE signs the grant agreement and receives the funds, which are then credited to the relevant collective.
4. **Documentation**\
   As always, the service agreement with the collective must be in place before receiving any funds.\
   All related documents must be stored in the Drive folder.

***

### Tax deductibility inside the EU

Open Collective Europe is **not authorized to issue tax-deductible receipts** for donations made by individuals within the EU.

This applies to:

* Direct donations via the platform (card, bank transfer)
* Manual contributions received from EU residents

{% hint style="warning" %}
We do not issue *attestations fiscales*, *Spendenbescheinigungen*, or similar national-level tax documents.
{% endhint %}

If a foundation or donor requires tax recognition in a specific EU country, we recommend exploring third-party fiscal sponsorship options or partnering with a local intermediary.

***

### Internal notes

* Each new foundation interaction should be tracked in Slack or GitBook if recurring.
* Flag any unusual legal conditions, reputational concerns, or restricted-use grants.
* When in doubt, escalate to the executive director or consult the compliance contact.


# Support Ops

{% hint style="danger" %}
**Before executing this process, you must go through** [**the Operational Checklist**](/oc-europe-internal-doc/support-ops/operational-checklist-mandatory-before-action)**.**
{% endhint %}

## Support Ops

This section introduces the internal operations and practices we follow to deliver high-quality support to hosted collectives, funders, and external stakeholders.

Open Collective Europe operates with a small team and a strong commitment to care, responsiveness, and legal compliance. Because we support a wide variety of collectives — many of which do not have formal structures — our support work often requires navigating nuanced legal, financial, and relational dynamics.

Support operations at OCE are not fully centralized: they rely on collaboration across roles (admin, ops, finance, and governance), and the ability to adapt to different funders, platforms, and jurisdictions.

This chapter includes:

* **Support Best Practices** – How we structure support tasks, how we use Freshdesk and Slack, and how we prioritize and escalate requests.
* **Application Review** – Our internal process for reviewing new collective applications and assigning the appropriate legal structure (Foundation or ASBL).

Questions about how we handle support can always be raised in `#ocef` or during weekly check-ins.


# Support Best Practices

***

{% hint style="danger" %}
**Before executing this process, you must go through** [**the Operational Checklist**](/oc-europe-internal-doc/support-ops/operational-checklist-mandatory-before-action)**.**
{% endhint %}

This page outlines our internal practices and expectations for providing effective and consistent support to collectives, funders, and partners.\
At OCE, support is a shared responsibility that spans financial operations, onboarding, legal coordination, and platform assistance.

### Our support philosophy

* **Clarity over speed** – We prioritize thoughtful, informed answers over quick responses.
* **Care over volume** – We don’t aim to “close tickets” quickly, but to build mutual understanding.
* **Transparency with boundaries** – We share as much as possible while protecting legal, financial, and compliance limits.

***

### Channels and tools

#### Freshdesk

* Main entry point for collective or donor support
* OCE handles Tier 2 tickets (complex expenses, grants, bank issues, tax questions)
* Use internal notes for cross-team context
* Tag tickets with `oce`, `legal`, `grant`, or other useful labels

{% hint style="warning" %}
Check FreshDesk daily for messages, and **always mark as Resolved** the ones you responded to.&#x20;
{% endhint %}

#### Slack (`#ocef` and private threads)

* Used for triaging, internal escalation, and follow-up
* Ask publicly unless private context is needed
* Document final decisions in GitBook if reusable

#### GitBook

* The single source of truth for recurring procedures, policies, and responses
* If you’ve answered a question more than twice → write it up

***

### Language & support quality

To ensure consistent and respectful communication, we rely on two main tools:

#### 1. Canned responses

We maintain a curated list of pre-written responses to common questions from hosted collectives. These ensure:

* Consistent messaging across team members
* Alignment with OCE policy
* Time-saving for routine inquiries

**Access**:\
To get access to the canned response library, contact **Ivan**.

You can also suggest new responses or edits if a situation comes up frequently.

<table data-header-hidden><thead><tr><th valign="top"></th><th valign="top"></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td valign="top">After the onboarding call OCEF</td><td valign="top">Hi,     Thank you for taking the time to speak with me earlier. I’d like to recap what we discussed about Open Collective Europe (OCE).      OCE is a Belgian foundation for public benefit focused on supporting impactful initiatives.  You can find our guidelines here and our technical documentation here.   To summarise it, here are a few key points :   Fundraising: OCE enables groups to raise money from various donors, including individuals, companies, and foundations.   Grant Applications: Your group is eligible to apply for grants. If you're applying for a grant, please inform us in advance so we can coordinate with the grantor to provide the necessary information and sign the grant agreement.   Responsibility: The collective's administrators are solely responsible for the collective's activities and management.   Expense policy:  This procedure applies for processing expenses.  Bank Accounts: Your collective cannot have external bank accounts. All funds must be held in your Open Collective account to ensure an accurate representation of your budget and activities.    Overhead costs:      8% for online (non-tax-deductible) donations.  10% for wire transfers, including grants from foundations and tax-deductible contributions from the US.   Lastly, feel free to join our Discord community to stay connected, share your progress, and engage with the broader Open Collective community.      Looking forward to seeing your initiative grow!  Best regards,</td></tr><tr><td valign="top">Paypal fees for low funds</td><td valign="top"> Hi,  Unfortunately your Collective does not have enough funds to cover for the fees of this payment method. Paypal reports its estimated PAYPAL fees on €XXXXXXX, but they are difficult to predict.  If you wish to proceed with PayPal, you could either use the "payee is covering the fees solution" if possible or manually adapt the fees and use the "empty balance" feature (the procedure is described here <a href="https://docs.opencollective.com/help/collectives/collective-settings/zero-collective-balance">https://docs.opencollective.com/help/collectives/collective-settings/zero-collective-balance</a>).  Otherwise, you can edit the payment method, select "New Bank Account" instead of "New Custom Payout Method" and write your coordinates in the form.  After that, please make sure to re-approve the expense.  Best regards, </td></tr><tr><td valign="top">Missing invoice</td><td valign="top"> Hi,  Thank you for submitting your expense.   To help us process this and get the funds to you as quickly as possible, we just need a small adjustment regarding the documentation.  Following the Belgian regulations, we can only pay expenses which include a valid fiscal document (like a tax receipt or a tax invoice).  While order confirmations are a great start, they unfortunately don't meet the legal requirements we must follow to protect the hosting infrastructure.  Could you please upload the final receipt or invoice to your expense? It’s simple to update it: - Click on the edit icon.  - Upload the document and save.   Once you've updated it, don't forget that a collective admin will need to approve the expense.   If you’re unsure where to find the right document or have any questions, feel free to check out our dedicated guide or just reply to this email.  Best regards, [Your Name] Open Collective Europe </td></tr><tr><td valign="top">Apply through platform</td><td valign="top"> Hi,  Thank you for applying to be fiscally hosted by Open Collective Europe.   We would like to ask you to fill in the application form here <a href="https://opencollective.com/europe/apply">https://opencollective.com/europe/apply</a> to continue with the application procedure.     For more information on the conditions, please check our documentation <a href="https://docs.opencollective.com/oceurope/">https://docs.opencollective.com/oceurope/</a>   Also, please bear in mind that there is a minimum of two identifiable admins needed for a collective to be approved.  If we don't hear from you within the next two weeks, we will not proceed with your application.  Best regards,   The OCE Team </td></tr><tr><td valign="top">Application rejection OSE</td><td valign="top"> Hi,     Thank you for your application to be fiscally hosted by Open Source Europe, and for taking the time to share your project with us.   After careful review, we have decided not to move forward with your application. Based on the information provided, we do not think the project is the right fit for OSE’s hosting framework at this stage.     We appreciate your interest and wish you all the best with the development of your initiative.     Best regards,  The OSE Team </td></tr><tr><td valign="top">Change payment to New Bank Account</td><td valign="top"> Hi,     In order to process this payment faster, would you please be able to edit the payment method, select "New Bank Account" instead of "New Custom Payout Method" and then write your coordinates in the form?     Thank you for your help.     Best regards, </td></tr><tr><td valign="top">Old Pending contribution - REGISTERED</td><td valign="top"> Hi,     Thank you for your email - it helped us identify the contribution. It is now registered on the collective's page.  Apologies for the delay, these are manual transactions and can slip under the radar sometimes in spite of our regular controls.     Because of that we recommend to use the card payment/PayPal whenever possible. It automates the procedure, arrives instantly to the collective's page and has a bit lower fee.     Best regards, </td></tr><tr><td valign="top">bank statement no</td><td valign="top"> Hi,  In order for the expense to be paid, we kindly ask you to provide a valid fiscal document - a receipt or an invoice. The bank statement is not considered as such by the tax authority. Could you please attach it and re-approve the expense?  For the general details on what the document should contain, please see our documentation here: <a href="https://docs.opencollective.com/oceurope/how-it-works/expenses">https://docs.opencollective.com/oceurope/how-it-works/expenses</a>  Best regards, </td></tr><tr><td valign="top">Paypal multicurrency no</td><td valign="top"> Hi,  would you please be able to submit the request in EUR and apply the today's exchange value? It is not possible for us to process multicurrency bills via Paypal.     To edit the expense, select More actions>Edit>  Thank you, Ivan  </td></tr><tr><td valign="top">Apply through form</td><td valign="top"> Hi,   Thank you for applying to Open Collective Europe.   To continue with your application, please complete our form at <a href="https://www.oceurope.org/apply">https://www.oceurope.org/apply</a>. This helps us better understand your collective and assess whether it’s a good fit for OCE.   You can also review our documentation here for more details on the conditions: <a href="https://docs.opencollective.com/oceurope/">https://docs.opencollective.com/oceurope/</a>   Please note that at least two identifiable admins are required for a collective to be approved.   We look forward to your submission.   Best regards,  The OCE Team    </td></tr><tr><td valign="top">Missing tax reference</td><td valign="top">Hi,     Thank you for submitting your expense!  It appears that in the attached invoice a few required details are missing, so we’re not able to move forward with the payout just yet.  To make sure the expense can be processed smoothly, could you please update the invoice to include the following information:    A unique invoice number    The full identification of the issuer (name, address, and tax or company registration number)    A clear description of the service provided    The applicable tax rate    If the tax rate is 0%, please include a short note directly on the invoice explaining the reason (for example: VAT exemption under Article YY of Law XX).      Please also note that the invoice should not mention Open Collective Europe    You can update the invoice directly by clicking on the edit icon on the expense. Once edited, the expense will need to be approved again by a collective admin.  If helpful, you can find more guidance on invoice requirements here: <a href="https://docs.opencollective.com/oceurope/how-it-works/expenses/invoices-or-reimbursements">https://docs.opencollective.com/oceurope/how-it-works/expenses/invoices-or-reimbursements</a>   Thank you for your help.  Best,</td></tr><tr><td valign="top">Closed for holidays</td><td valign="top">Season greetings! Reduced service for the holidays   We wanted to let you know in advance that we will be taking days off throughout the upcoming holiday season to spend some time on ourselves and with our families and friends.   We won't be reimbursing expenses between 25 December and 2 January 2. To ensure your expense request is processed in time, please submit it by 21 December 2023 EoB.  During this period, we may also take longer than usual to reply to your messages. If you have a query, feel free to check out our documentation, while we work through our support tickets. We’ll be back up to speed on 2 January taking care of any outstanding issues then.  Happy holidays!  The OCE Team</td></tr><tr><td valign="top">How to join OCE</td><td valign="top">How to join OCE     To transfer your collective from OCF to OCE, please do the following:      Spend down the balance in your OCF collective to $0. You can do this by paying out expenses or invoices or donating your remaining balance to another c3 organization.  Unhost your collective from OCF (steps outlined here).  Submit an application to be hosted by OCE here.</td></tr><tr><td valign="top">OCF Transition</td><td valign="top">Thank you for reaching out and for your consideration.     We appreciate your initiative and would be happy to host it.     We believe you have already looked at this introduction, particularly our expenses guidelines. If you haven't, please let us know if they work for you.     Regarding your collective's transition from OCF, please verify OCF's guidelines and contact them if you have any doubts or uncertainties about the obligations between your collective and their organization defined by their Terms and Conditions.     If you have already decided to transfer your collective from OCF to OCE:    You have to spend down the balance in your OCF collective to $0. You can do this by paying out expenses or invoices or requesting that your balance be moved to OCE (remember that this option might take time).  Unhost your collective from OCF (steps outlined here).  Submit an application to be hosted by OCE here.      We would also welcome you if you decided to create a brand new collective and immediately start receiving donations.  To do this, please read our documentation and apply to OCE here. Please respect the terms and conditions of the agreement you have in place with OCF, which does not allow one initiative to have two fiscal hosts (e.g., the new collective cannot be a duplicate of the old one).      Feel free to reach out if you have specific questions.  Our tech team is working on solutions to allow the collectives to export all their data and keep them after the dissolution of OCF. Here, you can find their latest update.     We look forward to hearing from you.     Best regards,</td></tr><tr><td valign="top">OOO Generic</td><td valign="top">Hi,  Thank you for reaching out. I am currently out of the office and will not be able to respond to emails until [Date of Return].  During this time, I will have limited access to email. If your matter is urgent, please contact [Alternative Contact Person/Department] at [Alternative Contact Information].  For all other inquiries, I will respond to your email as soon as possible upon my return.  Thank you for your understanding.  Best regards, [Your Name]</td></tr><tr><td valign="top">Post check-in: add a second admin</td><td valign="top">Hi,  we appreciate the latest update you posted and note that your collective is still active.  We wish you all the best in continuing its activity and would like to ask you to add a second identifiable admin to your collective.  It helps the transparency of your activity and ensures its continuity. It is also a mandatory requirement on OCE.  Don't hesitate to reach out to us at <a href="mailto:oce@opencollective.Com">oce@opencollective.Com</a> if you have any questions or doubts.   Kind regards, The OCE Team</td></tr><tr><td valign="top">Application rejection - no response to our questions OCEF</td><td valign="top"> Hi,     Thank you for your application to be fiscally hosted by Open Collective Europe.     We followed up a few weeks ago with some additional questions, but have not received a response. Since we need this information in order to continue reviewing your application, we will close your application for now.     You are welcome to get back in touch at any time if you would like to resume the process.     We wish you all the best with your project.     Best regards, The OCE Team </td></tr><tr><td valign="top">KYC legal name</td><td valign="top"> Hi,     Thank you for submitting the expense.   To help us process this and get the funds to you as quickly as possible, we just need a small adjustment.     Could you please enter your full legal name in the “Legal Name” section of your Profile settings?  As the information in that section is not public, this will allow you to continue using your alias for public purposes while ensuring we meet our internal compliance requirements.     In line with Belgian anti-money laundering (AML) regulations and our Know Your Customer (KYC) obligations, we are required to have the legal name of any individual receiving an expense payout.     Thank you for your help and understanding.  Best regards, </td></tr><tr><td valign="top">No news 2 weeks rejection</td><td valign="top">Hi,  We haven’t heard back from you after our last message, so we’ll assume you’ve decided not to move forward with the application for now.  We wish you the best with your project, and if you ever decide to reapply in the future, you’re more than welcome to reach out.  All the best, The OCE Team</td></tr><tr><td valign="top">No crypto</td><td valign="top">Hi,  Due to the strict AML requirements imposed by our banking providers and the Belgian tax authorities, OCEF cannot reimburse expenses where the primary documentation is denominated in cryptocurrency. It poses a serious risk of our bank accounts being flagged as "high risk", which may lead to their closing.   For the time being, we can only pay out invoices and receipts denominated in fiat currency (EUR, USD, etc.), with complete tax information of the provider, to ensure fiscal traceability.</td></tr><tr><td valign="top">No reimbursement in a B2B exchange</td><td valign="top">Hi,  Thank you for submitting your request.  Please note that it is unfortunately not possible to use the Collective’s funds to pay or reimburse expenses incurred by a registered company, such as a B2B invoice issued by one business to another.  For this expense to be eligible, the invoice would need to be issued directly to the Collective.  If you are acting on behalf of the business, you are welcome to issue a B2C invoice addressed to the Collective instead, including an individual admin's address, and VAT where applicable, and we’ll be happy to review it.  For reference, please don’t hesitate to consult our guidelines here: <a href="https://docs.opencollective.com/oceurope/how-it-works/expenses">https://docs.opencollective.com/oceurope/how-it-works/expenses</a>.  Please, let us know if you have any questions.  Best regards,</td></tr><tr><td valign="top">PayPal verify / avoid</td><td valign="top"> Hi,  Thank you for submitting the expense.  We need just a small correction from your side to finish processing it.  Due to recent changes in PayPal operations, processing payouts to PayPal accounts has become a completely manual task for us.
